elements.sent-2411-002: The main goal of this paper is to benchmark all publicly available logic-based and learning-based algorithms for the noise filtering/removal task on sparse satellite data.
elements.sent-2411-003: The best-performing algorithm effectively denoises the sparse, noisy event stream while fully preserving the satellite signals.
elements.sent-2411-004: In addition, this paper proposes a new noise filtering algorithm, Cross-Convolution, and demonstrate the noise filtering capability of FEAST algorithm~\cite{afshar_event-based_2020 with and without a classifier.. ].}
elements.sent-2411-005: Event cameras capture sparse, asynchronous brightness changes which offer high temporal resolution, high dynamic range, low power consumption, and sparse data output.
elements.sent-2411-006: These advantages make them ideal for Space Situational Awareness, particularly in detecting resident space objects moving within a telescope's field of view.
elements.sent-2411-007: However, the output from event cameras often includes substantial background activity noise, which is known to be more prevalent in low-light conditions.
elements.sent-2411-008: This noise can overwhelm the sparse events generated by satellite signals, making detection and tracking more challenging.
elements.sent-2411-009: Existing noise-filtering algorithms struggle in these scenarios because they are typically designed for denser scenes, where losing some signal is acceptable.
elements.sent-2411-010: This limitation hinders the application of event cameras in complex, real-world environments where signals are extremely sparse.
elements.sent-2411-011: In this paper, we propose new event-driven noise-filtering algorithms specifically designed for very sparse scenes.
elements.sent-2411-012: We categorise the algorithms into logical-based and learning-based approaches and benchmark their performance against 11 state-of-the-art noise-filtering algorithms, evaluating how effectively they remove noise and hot pixels while preserving the signal.
elements.sent-2411-013: Their performance was quantified by measuring signal retention and noise removal accuracy, with results reported using ROC curves across the parameter space.
elements.sent-2411-014: Additionally, we introduce a new high-resolution satellite dataset with ground truth from a real-world platform under various noise conditions, which we have made publicly available.
elements.sent-2411-015: Code, dataset, and trained weights are available at \url{https://github.com/samiarja/dvs_sparse_filter .}
elements.sent-2411-016: Event cameras, also known as Dynamic Vision Sensors (DVS) \cite{lichtsteiner_128times128_2008,Finateu2020510A1 , are optical sensors inspired by biological eyes.}
elements.sent-2411-017: Unlike traditional cameras that capture the global brightness at regular intervals, DVS cameras detect changes in brightness at each pixel asynchronously, reporting these changes as log-intensity changes.
elements.sent-2411-018: These capabilities allow them to acquire visual information at the same rate as the scene dynamics, offering extremely high temporal resolution, low data rate output and a high dynamic range.
elements.sent-2411-019: Because of these features, DVS cameras can respond rapidly to dynamic scenes, making them ideal for satellite survey observations~\cite{afshar_event-based_ssa .}
elements.sent-2411-020: The output of a Dynamic Vision Sensor (DVS) often contains a significant amount of undesirable background activity noise, which can blend with the actual signal and limit its effectiveness in practical applications.
elements.sent-2411-021: These background activity noise events originate from pixels even in the absence of any scene activity, rendering them noninformative.
elements.sent-2411-022: Background activity noise is primarily caused by photon and electron shot noise, as well as leakage in the reset transistor~\cite{graca_optimal_2023 .}
elements.sent-2411-023: Notably, the noise rate increases dramatically under low-light conditions.
elements.sent-2411-024: The noise performance of a DVS depends not only on scene illumination but also on the camera's user-controllable bias settings.
elements.sent-2411-025: Therefore, adjusting the bias can potentially improve the clarity of the received signal~\cite{Graca_2023_CVPR,Mcreynolds2023ExploitingAD,nozaki_temperature_2017,graca_unraveling_2021 .}
elements.sent-2411-026: However, large portions of the signal may still be compromised by noise from event jitter, quantization noise~\cite{Hu_2021_CVPR and hot pixels (i.e. pixels with an abnormally high firing rate), which decrease the Signal-to-Noise Ratio (SNR) and increase the data rate~\cite{guo_low_2023,Hu_2021_CVPR .}}
elements.sent-2411-027: Noise filtering the event stream at the camera output improves the SNR and reduces computational demands, thereby decreasing power consumption by orders of magnitude.
elements.sent-2411-028: To estimate the motion vector within the data, we utilised the Contrast Maximization (CMax) framework~\cite{gallego_unifying_2018 , which generates a motion landscape representing event activity across the velocity fields.}
elements.sent-2411-029: In Figures~\ref{fig:denoisingmotivation (b), the CMax-generated motion landscape reveals a peak at zero speed ($v_x = 0 \ [\text{px/s ]$, $v_y = 0 \ [\text{px/s ]$).}}}
elements.sent-2411-030: This peak corresponds to unwanted background activity noise, indicating a concentration of stationary events in the image plane.
elements.sent-2411-031: An effective noise filtering algorithm should eliminate this peak by removing only the background activity noise, thereby enabling accurate motion compensation, as shown in Figure~\ref{fig:denoisingmotivation (c).}

elements.sent-2411-032: In the broader neuromorphic and computer vision communities, eliminating unwanted noise to enhance visual data quality is referred to by various terms such as "denoising," "noise removal," "background activity filtering," and "noise filtering." In this paper, we use the term noise filtering to describe this process.
elements.sent-2411-033: Despite different terminology, the ultimate goal is the same: to identify and remove noisy events from the data stream.
elements.sent-2411-034: The key distinction among approaches lies in problem formulation.
elements.sent-2411-035: Some methods treat noise filtering as a binary classification task, requiring learning and large datasets; we refer to these as learning-based methods.
elements.sent-2411-036: Others simplify it to statistical calculations based on heuristics and specified conditions; we call these logical-based methods.
elements.sent-2411-037: In this paper, we formulate the noise filtering task as a multi-step statistical calculation for the CrossConv algorithm (i.e. logical-based) and as a binary classification task for the FEAST algorithm (i.e. learning-based).
elements.sent-2411-038: This paper focuses on events produced by satellites which are the least dominant and the most valuable information for satellite surveying.
elements.sent-2411-039: Unlike stars, satellites are more trivial to label since they are usually isolated and move in the opposite direction from the telescope.
elements.sent-2411-040: Additionally, determining the limiting magnitude of star brightness from an event camera remains an open research question~\cite{marcireau2023binocular,ralph_astrometric_2023,mcreynolds_demystifying_2023 , as faint stars often blend with noise, making them difficult to identify and label.}
elements.sent-2411-041: For this reason, our evaluation of the noise-filtering algorithms is based solely on how well they preserve satellite events.
elements.sent-2411-042: In this paper, we investigate whether noise filtering is ideal for very sparse satellite data and, if so, whether logical-based or learning-based approaches should be employed.
elements.sent-2411-043: We define logical-based algorithms as those that use deterministic rules to distinguish signal events from noise events.
elements.sent-2411-044: In contrast, learning-based algorithms learn features of the signal and noise during a training phase and perform noise filtering during inference.
elements.sent-2411-045: Prior noise filtering algorithms include bioinspired filter~\cite{barrios2018less , hardware-based filter~\cite{khodamoradi_on-space_2018,liu2015design , spatial filter~\cite{benosman2013event,delbruck_frame-free_2008 and temporal filter~\cite{baldwin_edncnn,karray_inceptive_2019,wang2020joint,fang_aednet_2022 , aiming to achieve real-time processing.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-046: While effective in some scenarios, they often struggle with the low SNR in data from small, sparse objects like satellites.
elements.sent-2411-047: These methods may inadvertently remove signal events along with noise, which is undesirable for precise applications such as satellite tracking.
elements.sent-2411-048: To ensure a fair comparison between noise filtering algorithms, we first established a high-quality ground truth and employed the evaluation strategy from~\cite{guo_low_2023 , using Receiver Operating Characteristic (ROC) curves across a wide parameter space for each algorithm.}

elements.sent-2411-050: We also evaluated how long a recording needs to be for each algorithm to achieve maximum noise-filtering performance.

elements.sent-2411-053: We also evaluated the noise filtering capabilities of the learning-based FEAST~\cite{afshar_event-based_2020 algorithm (Feature Extraction with Adaptive Selection Thresholds).}
elements.sent-2411-054: The contribution of this work can be summarised as follows:.
elements.sent-2411-055: We constructed an event-based dataset of satellites called "Ev-Satellites" with high-quality ground truth to enable precise noise filtering evaluation.
elements.sent-2411-056: We proposed a novel logical-based algorithm called CrossConv and evaluated the performance of the learning-based FEAST~\cite{afshar_event-based_2020 algorithm, which had not been previously assessed for noise-filtering tasks.}
elements.sent-2411-057: We conducted an extensive comparison against 11 state-of-the-art noise-filtering methods, rigorously quantifying their performance using metrics such as Signal Retain (SR), Noise Removal (NR), Denoise Accuracy (DA), Hot Pixel Removal (HPR) and Receiver Operating Characteristic (ROC) curve.

elements.sent-2411-152: Below we describe our algorithms.. Cross-Convolution.

elements.sent-2411-215: To evaluate the performance of our proposed algorithms: CrossConv, FEAST, and FEAST+classifier, we benchmarked them against 11 publicly available noise filtering algorithms.
elements.sent-2411-216: Below, we provide an overview of each of these baseline methods.. KNoise~\cite{khodamoradi_on-space_2018 .}
elements.sent-2411-217: It maintains two memory buffers to store the most recent events for each row and column in the sensor array.
elements.sent-2411-219: For each new event, it computes the Manhattan distances to these previous events and identifies the minimum distance $D_{\text{min }$.}
elements.sent-2411-220: If $D_{\text{min }$ exceeds a predefined threshold, the event is considered noise and discarded.}
elements.sent-2411-222: It applies the same distance-based filtering rules as FWF within each window, enhancing the discrimination between signal and noise.
elements.sent-2411-225: For noise filtering, it compares the timestamp of the current event with those of neighboring events within a certain radius.
elements.sent-2411-226: Events are retained if the temporal difference is below a pre-defined threshold, ensuring that only temporally coherent events are passed through.
elements.sent-2411-228: It then filters out events with abnormal flow values, based on the assumption that noise events will not conform to the consistent motion patterns of the scene.. YNoise~\cite{feng_event_2020 .}
elements.sent-2411-229: Events located in high-density regions are considered part of moving edge and are retained, while those in low-density regions are filtered out as noise.
elements.sent-2411-231: Inceptive events are triggered by edges or corners in the scene and are detected based on their temporal isolation from preceding events.
elements.sent-2411-232: Scaling events, which represent the magnitude of the intensity change but provide less structural information, are discarded.
elements.sent-2411-233: The algorithm focuses on retaining inceptive events and considers scaling events as noise.
elements.sent-2411-235: It learns spatiotemporal structural patterns from the data to discriminate between noise and signal events.
elements.sent-2411-236: The algorithm operates by extracting a patch of the nearest neighbouring pixels around each new event.
elements.sent-2411-237: These patches are processed using a linear decay kernel to capture temporal information, and the resulting features are fed into the classifier.. AEDAT~\cite{fang_aednet_2022 .}
elements.sent-2411-238: It applies one-dimensional convolutions to neighbouring pixels along the time dimension, capturing the spatiotemporal connectivity of events while preserving their asynchronous nature.

elements.sent-2411-260: To further evaluate the performance of all algorithms, we looked at the ROC curve and picked the parameter that gave the lowest FPR and the highest TPR to measure Signal Retain (SR), Noise Removal (NR), Hot Pixels Removal (HPR) and Denoising Accuracy (DA)~\cite{duan_led_2024 as well as the Area Under Curve (AUC).}
elements.sent-2411-261: The metrics are described as follows: \begin{equation}TPR = \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{N} \left( d_i = 1 \land g_i = 1 \right)}{\sum_{i=1}^{N} \left( g_i = 1 \right)} \\\end{equation} \begin{equation}FPR = \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{N} \left( d_i = 1 \land g_i = 0 \right)}{\sum_{i=1}^{N} \left( g_i = 0 \right)}\end{equation} \begin{equation}SR = 100 \times \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{N} \left( d_i = 1 \land g_i = 1 \right)}{\sum_{i=1}^{N} \left( g_i = 1 \right)} \\\end{equation} \begin{equation}NR = 100 \times \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{N} \left( d_i = 0 \land g_i = 0 \right)}{\sum_{i=1}^{N} \left( g_i = 0 \right)} \\\end{equation} \begin{equation}HPR = 100 \times \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{N} \left( d_i = 0 \land g_i = 2 \right)}{\sum_{i=1}^{N} \left( g_i = 2 \right)} \\\end{equation} \begin{equation}DA = \frac{SR+NR+HPR}{3}\end{equation} where $\land$ denotes the logical AND operation, $N$ total number of events, $d_i$ is the noise filtering output with 1s represent satellites events and 0s represent noise+hot pixels events, and $g_i$ is ground truth with 0s represent the noise events, 1s represents satellite events and 2s represents hot pixels.

elements.sent-2411-284: Figure~\ref{fig:qualitativeresults illustrates the performance of each noise filtering algorithm across three test datasets from the Ev-Satellite dataset.}
elements.sent-2411-285: Rows 1 to 4, show multiple satellites moving in the same direction.
elements.sent-2411-286: Rows 5 to 8, show a single satellite with a non-linear motion from the wind.
elements.sent-2411-287: Rows 9 to 12 show a single faint satellite among dense stars.
elements.sent-2411-288: Among the algorithms, FEAST+classifier consistently outperforms the other algorithms, demonstrating superior noise and hot pixel removal capabilities.
elements.sent-2411-289: In contrast, EvFlow's performance varies across different recordings, indicating inconsistency over multiple recordings.
elements.sent-2411-290: STDF, CrossConv, and AEDAT tend to ignore a higher number of noise events, while IETS and YNoise often remove too many satellite events, resulting in higher false negatives.
elements.sent-2411-291: Overall, FEAST+classifier proves to be the most effective in reducing noise and preserving relevant events.
elements.figure-2411-008: \begin{subfigure}{\linewidth} \includegraphics[width=\linewidth]{Figures/windowsize_SR_subplots.png} \caption{Signal Retain (SR) metric in \%.} \label{fig:windowsize_NR} \end{subfigure} \begin{subfigure}{\linewidth} \includegraphics[width=\linewidth]{Figures/windowsize_NR_subplots.png} \caption{Noise Removal (NR) metric in \%.} \label{fig:windowsize_SR} \end{subfigure} \caption{Performance comparison of all noise filtering algorithms over window sizes ranging from 1 to 20 seconds. The performance of each algorithm is presented in its own plot because of the significant differences among each methods, and to avoids the need to zoom in on individual curves.} \label{fig:windowsize}
elements.sent-2411-293: We evaluated the performance of various noise filtering algorithms across different window sizes to determine the optimal event window length for accurate noise filtering.
elements.sent-2411-294: Using Receiver Operating Characteristic (ROC) curves, we selected parameters that yielded maximum accuracy while keeping all other parameters constant.
elements.sent-2411-295: We systematically varied the window size from 1 to 20 seconds, incrementing by 1 second at each step.
elements.sent-2411-296: Additionally, we applied a stride such that, for a given window size $w_s=1$ second, the stride length was set to $w_s/5$.
elements.sent-2411-297: This striding was performed from the beginning to the end of the recording, and we calculated the average accuracy for each window size.
elements.sent-2411-298: This procedure was repeated for all window sizes using both the Signal Retain (SR) and Noise Removal (NR) metrics.
elements.sent-2411-299: The results of this experiment are summarized in Figure~\ref{fig:windowsize .}
elements.sent-2411-300: Our findings suggest that increasing the window size enhances noise filtering performance for logical-based algorithms, likely because larger windows provide more samples for the algorithms to process.
elements.sent-2411-301: This improvement was observed exclusively in the logical-based algorithms.
elements.sent-2411-302: In contrast, we observed that the noise removal performance decreased with larger window sizes for the IETS, STCF, and YNoise algorithms, while their signal retention performance increased.
elements.sent-2411-303: This indicates that these algorithms denoise less effectively with larger windows.
elements.sent-2411-304: On the contrary, the performance of the learning-based algorithm remained largely unchanged and without a clear upward or downward trend when the window size increases.
elements.sent-2411-305: Among all the algorithms tested, the combination of FEAST with a classifier achieved the best noise removal and signal retention performance.
elements.sent-2411-306: This was followed by the CrossConv algorithm, which demonstrated high signal retention but was less effective in noise removal since it prioritises hot pixels.
elements.sent-2411-307: The TS algorithm emerged as the second-best denoiser overall, providing better noise removal at the expense of removing more signal information.
elements.sent-2411-308: Filtering the noise in sparse event streams can reduce visual noise but comes at the cost of removing real satellite events.
elements.sent-2411-309: In this work, the best algorithm can only preserve up to 95.98\% of the satellite events.
elements.sent-2411-310: In very sparse satellite streams, filtering the noise might remove significant portions of actual data, which can be undesirable.
elements.sent-2411-311: Hot pixels are the most dominant type of noise which artificially increases the event rate, especially in sparse scenes.
elements.sent-2411-312: This makes the task of motion estimation (e.g., CMax) and tracking~\cite{ralph2022real more challenging, therefore, their removal is more critical than background noise.}
elements.sent-2411-313: However, most noise filtering algorithms remove hot pixels in pre- or post-processing or by aggressive noise filtering, which can reduce true positives.
elements.sent-2411-314: We found that KNoise was the most effective at removing hot pixels among the logical-based algorithm, followed by our proposed lightweight CrossConv algorithm.
elements.sent-2411-315: Thus, in very sparse scenes, algorithms that prioritize hot pixel removal are most beneficial.
elements.sent-2411-316: Noise filtering also assists in other SSA tasks like determining the limiting magnitude.
elements.sent-2411-317: Previous work determined magnitude by merging event streams using a binocular event camera setup~\cite{marcireau2023binocular .}
elements.sent-2411-318: Our study provides insights into noise filtering from a single event stream and serves as a comparison to the binocular approach.
elements.sent-2411-319: Given the vast data from Astrosite, developing end-to-end, data-driven noise filtering algorithms that exploit structural cues is practical.

elements.sent-2411-327: In this paper, we presented three algorithms that achieve high-accuracy noise filtering on a high-resolution and real-world satellite dataset.
elements.sent-2411-328: Our primary goal was to address the challenge of noise filtering of very sparse satellite signals that can be obscured by excessive noise and hot pixels, demonstrating the best approach to filter noise from the event data while preserving the satellite signals.
elements.sent-2411-329: We conducted extensive experiments using 11 state-of-the-art noise filtering methods and provided a comparative analysis of their performance.
elements.sent-2411-330: We utilized the ROC curve for performance comparison over the entire parameter range to ensure a fair comparison between the algorithms.
elements.sent-2411-331: The experimental results highlight the superiority of the learning-based FEAST algorithm in removing noise and hot pixels.
elements.sent-2411-332: Additionally, the logical-based EvFlow algorithm proved most effective in preserving satellite signals when using appropriate parameters.
elements.sent-2411-333: We hope that our work will contribute valuable insights to the event community and spur future research on event-based sparse noise filtering for SSA applications.. \setlength{\parskip {10pt Acknowledgements.}}
